mcaWeights.note: Weights must sum to 1.0. Adjust here to change MCA importance of each criterion.

criteria.title: Soil Management
criteria.description: How you manage tillage, soil disturbance and surface cover in your citrus orchard.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Citrus & Fruit Trees; Khangura et al. 2023

questions.text: How do you manage tillage in your orchard?

options.label: Regular ploughing / disc harrowing that turns the soil
Occasional shallow tillage (>5 cm), several times per year
Minimal surface tillage only in specific justified situations
No tillage at all – soil is never turned or inverted

questions.text: What covers the soil between your citrus trees throughout the year?

options.label: Bare soil – kept clear by herbicides or tillage
Partial cover, mainly in winter / wet season
Permanent organic mulch (wood chips, pruning residues, straw)
Living plant cover (cover crops / spontaneous vegetation) maintained year-round

questions.text: How would you describe the soil structure when you dig a handful?

options.label: Completely compacted and hard – difficult to penetrate
Mostly compacted with some clumps
Moderately loose with some structure and aggregates visible
Loose, crumbly, rich in earthworms and visible biological activity

criteria.title: Water Management
criteria.description: How efficiently and regeneratively you manage water on your Valencia citrus farm.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 2 & Citrus section

questions.text: How do you irrigate your citrus trees?

options.label: Flood or furrow irrigation without scheduling
Sprinkler or surface drip on a fixed timed schedule
Localised drip irrigation with some efficiency monitoring
Precision drip with soil moisture sensors or night-time scheduling to minimise evaporation

questions.text: Do you capture and retain rainwater on your farm?

options.label: No – water runs off the farm freely
Some terracing or berms but no active harvesting
Terraces, swales or contour bunds that slow and infiltrate runoff
Pond, reservoir or infiltration trenches actively harvest and store rainwater

questions.text: Where does the water for your farm come from?

options.label: Primarily deep aquifer without monitoring or limits
Aquifer with some monitoring, no reduction plan
Mix of aquifer and surface / canal water within legal rights
Primarily surface water or rainwater; aquifer use is minimal and monitored

criteria.title: Biodiversity
criteria.description: How you protect and actively increase wild biodiversity on and around your farm.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 3 & Citrus section; Jayasinghe et al. 2023

questions.text: Are there hedges, flower strips or ecological corridors on or around your farm?

options.label: None – boundaries are bare or concrete
Some existing natural vegetation on margins, not managed for biodiversity
Hedges or flower strips present on at least one side of the farm
Multiple hedges, flower strips and ecological corridors connecting with surrounding landscape

questions.text: What habitats exist for pollinators and natural pest controllers on your farm?

options.label: None – no specific habitats maintained
Some flowering weeds tolerated but no deliberate habitat
Flower strips or insect refuges installed in at least one area
Multiple habitats: flower strips, insect hotels, ponds or shelters for beneficial fauna actively managed

questions.text: Have you noticed a change in biodiversity (birds, insects, earthworms) on your farm in recent years?

options.label: Clear decline – fewer species and individuals observed
No noticeable change
Slight increase in some species (e.g. more earthworms or beneficial insects)
Clear increase – more birds, pollinators, earthworms and soil life observed

criteria.title: Plant Health & Inputs
criteria.description: Whether you use synthetic pesticides and fertilisers, and how you manage pests and disease.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 8 & Citrus plant health; Khangura et al. 2023

questions.text: How do you control pests and diseases in your citrus?

options.label: Regular preventive spraying with synthetic pesticides / fungicides on a fixed calendar
Synthetic pesticides used only when pest threshold is reached
Mainly organic / biological products; synthetic only as last resort in justified cases
No synthetic pesticides – biological control, pheromone traps, beneficial insects and preventive ecosystem management only

questions.text: How do you manage weeds in your orchard?

options.label: Regular herbicide applications to keep soil bare
Herbicides used occasionally, combined with mechanical mowing
Primarily mechanical management; herbicides rarely or never used
No herbicides – weeds managed through cover crops, mulching or selective mowing

questions.text: How do you fertilise your citrus trees?

options.label: Primarily synthetic mineral fertilisers (NPK, urea) via fertigation or broadcasting
Mix of synthetic and organic fertilisers
Mainly organic fertilisers (compost, manure); synthetic used occasionally
Only organic / biological inputs: compost, fermented manure, bokashi, biofertilisers – no synthetics

criteria.title: Soil Nutrition & Biology
criteria.description: How actively you feed the soil ecosystem and promote microbial life.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Citrus soil management; Khangura et al. 2023 – soil biology

questions.text: How do you return organic matter to your soil?

options.label: Pruning residues and plant material are removed or burned
Some organic matter left but no active composting or amendment
Pruning residues chipped and left as mulch; some compost applied
Systematic return of all organic matter: chipped prunings as mulch, compost and organic amendments applied regularly

questions.text: Do you use biological or microbial products to support soil life?

options.label: No – I rely only on mineral fertilisers
Occasionally use organic foliar sprays
Use mycorrhizal inoculants, biostimulants or fermented preparations (compost tea, bokashi)
Regular programme with mycorrhizae, biofertilisers, fermented preparations and natural minerals to actively build the soil microbiome

questions.text: Do you use cover crops with legumes to naturally improve soil nitrogen?

options.label: No cover crops – soil kept bare between trees
Cover crops used but no legumes included
Mix including some legumes (vetch, clover) for nitrogen fixation
Diverse cover crop mix with legumes, grasses and native plants – designed to improve fertility and water infiltration

criteria.title: Vegetation & Cover Management
criteria.description: How you manage the vegetation between trees, with or without livestock.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criteria 4 & 6; Citrus section

questions.text: How do you manage the vegetation growing between your citrus trees?

options.label: Eliminated with herbicides or frequent mowing to bare soil
Mowed mechanically several times per year with no further management
Selective minimal mowing; vegetation allowed to grow and cycle naturally
Managed with planned grazing (sheep / goats) or very selective minimal mechanical management

questions.text: Do you integrate livestock in your citrus orchard?

options.label: No livestock – not considered
Occasional unmanaged grazing
Planned seasonal grazing with defined entry / exit times
Fully planned rotational grazing integrated into orchard management with appropriate stocking rates

questions.text: How do you manage pruning residues from your citrus trees?

options.label: Burned or removed from the farm
Left in piles on the ground without processing
Chipped and left on soil surface as mulch
Chipped as mulch and / or composted; some used as animal feed where possible

criteria.title: Crop Diversification
criteria.description: Whether you grow only citrus monoculture or integrate other plants and varieties.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 7; Khangura et al. 2023 – crop diversity

questions.text: Do you grow other crops or plants alongside your citrus trees?

options.label: Pure monoculture – only citrus, nothing else
A few other fruit trees but nothing systematic
Some intercropping or agroforestry elements (other fruit trees, shrubs)
Diverse agroforestry or intercropping system with multiple species integrated in time and space

questions.text: What types of citrus varieties do you grow?

options.label: Single commercial variety, highly dependent on external inputs
One or two commercial varieties
Multiple varieties including some traditional / local ones
Diverse varieties including traditional, local and disease-resistant varieties adapted to the Valencia microclimate

questions.text: Do you use companion plants or trap crops to manage pests naturally?

options.label: No – I rely only on chemical pest control
Occasionally some flowering plants but not strategically
Some trap crops or companion plants used for specific pests (e.g. marigolds, radishes)
Strategic use of companion plants, trap crops and refuge flowers throughout the orchard for ecological pest balance

criteria.title: Plastics & Waste
criteria.description: How you minimise and responsibly manage plastic use and farm waste.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 9 & Citrus materials section

questions.text: How much single-use plastic do you use on your farm (nets, drip tape, trays, bags)?

options.label: Heavy use of single-use plastics with no reduction plan
Moderate use; some reuse but mainly single-use
Actively reducing plastics; reusable alternatives used where possible
Minimal plastics; reusable, recyclable or biodegradable materials used throughout the farm

questions.text: How do you dispose of agricultural plastics (drip tape, packaging, old nets)?

options.label: Burned on farm or left in the field
Left in storage with no proper disposal plan
Taken to municipal waste point occasionally
Systematic delivery to a certified plastics management company with documented traceability

criteria.title: Planning & Monitoring
criteria.description: Whether your practices are deliberately adapted to your local context and evolving over time.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 1; Jayasinghe et al. 2023

questions.text: Do you have a written or structured plan for improving your farm's regenerative practices?

options.label: No plan – farming as always done
Some informal ideas but no structured plan
A clear improvement plan with priorities identified
A documented long-term regenerative management plan with monitoring and annual review

questions.text: Do you regularly observe and record changes on your farm (soil, pests, water, biodiversity)?

options.label: No systematic observation
Some informal observation but nothing recorded
Regular observation with some informal records or photos
Systematic monitoring: soil tests, photo points, biodiversity records, water use tracked annually

questions.text: How do you stay updated on regenerative farming knowledge?

options.label: No active learning – rely on existing practice
Occasional reading or advice from cooperative
Attend courses or farm visits regularly
Active member of a learning network; participates in research, trials or knowledge exchange with scientists and farmers

criteria.title: Social & Community
criteria.description: Your participation in local community, cooperative networks and knowledge sharing.
criteria.source: Iberian RA Criteria 2026 – Criterion 10 & Citrus socioeconomic aspects

questions.text: Are you part of any cooperative, farmers' network or regenerative agriculture group?

options.label: No – farm independently with no network involvement
Member of a cooperative but only for commercial purposes
Active in a cooperative or farmers group with some knowledge exchange
Active member of a regenerative agriculture network; collaborates on learning, trials and collective improvement

questions.text: How do you sell your citrus?

options.label: Exclusively through large-scale distributors with no connection to end consumer
Mainly distributors, with occasional local sales
Mix of local / direct sales and distributor channels
Significant direct or local sales (farm shop, local market, short supply chain, Km 0)

questions.text: Do you share knowledge or open your farm to others (visits, events, social media)?

options.label: No sharing – keep practices private
Occasionally talk to neighbours about farming
Participate in farm visits or events, or share some information publicly
Actively share progress through social media, open farm days, or collaborations with schools or research centres
